1 --- 2 title: Route Handlers 3 nav_title: Route Handlers 4 description: Learn how to use Route Handlers 5 related: 6 title: API Reference 7 description: Learn more about Route Handlers 8 links: 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/route 10 - app/guides/backend-for-frontend 11 --- 12 13 ## Route Handlers 14 15 Route Handlers allow you to create custom request handlers for a given route using the Web [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs. 16 17 24 25 > **Good to know**: Route Handlers are only available inside the `app` directory. They are the equivalent of [API Routes](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/api-routes) inside the `pages` directory meaning you **do not** need to use API Routes and Route Handlers together. 26 27 ### Convention 28 29 Route Handlers are defined in a [`route.js|ts` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) inside the `app` directory: 30 31 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 32 export async function GET(request: Request) {} 33 ``` 34 35 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 36 export async function GET(request) {} 37 ``` 38 39 Route Handlers can be nested anywhere inside the `app` directory, similar to `page.js` and `layout.js`. But there **cannot** be a `route.js` file at the same route segment level as `page.js`. 40 41 ### Supported HTTP Methods 42 43 The following [HTTP methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods) are supported: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, and `OPTIONS`. If an unsupported method is called, Next.js will return a `405 Method Not Allowed` response. 44 45 ### Extended `NextRequest` and `NextResponse` APIs 46 47 In addition to supporting the native [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs, Next.js extends them with [`NextRequest`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request) and [`NextResponse`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-response) to provide convenient helpers for advanced use cases. 48 49 ### Caching 50 51 Route Handlers are not cached by default. You can, however, opt into caching for `GET` methods. Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached. To cache a `GET` method, use a [route config option](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#dynamic) such as `export const dynamic = 'force-static'` in your Route Handler file. 52 53 ```ts filename="app/items/route.ts" switcher 54 export const dynamic = 'force-static' 55 56 export async function GET() { 57 const res = await fetch('https://data.mongodb-api.com/...', { 58 headers: { 59 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 60 'API-Key': process.env.DATA_API_KEY, 61 }, 62 }) 63 const data = await res.json() 64 65 return Response.json({ data }) 66 } 67 ``` 68 69 ```js filename="app/items/route.js" switcher 70 export const dynamic = 'force-static' 71 72 export async function GET() { 73 const res = await fetch('https://data.mongodb-api.com/...', { 74 headers: { 75 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 76 'API-Key': process.env.DATA_API_KEY, 77 }, 78 }) 79 const data = await res.json() 80 81 return Response.json({ data }) 82 } 83 ``` 84 85 > **Good to know**: Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached, even if they are placed alongside a `GET` method that is cached, in the same file. 86 87 #### With Cache Components 88 89 When [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) is enabled, `GET` Route Handlers follow the same model as normal UI routes in your application. They run at request time by default, can be prerendered when they don't access dynamic or runtime data, and you can use `use cache` to include dynamic data in the static response. 90 91 **Static example** - doesn't access dynamic or runtime data, so it will be prerendered at build time: 92 93 ```tsx filename="app/api/project-info/route.ts" 94 export async function GET() { 95 return Response.json({ 96 projectName: 'Next.js', 97 }) 98 } 99 ``` 100 101 **Dynamic example** - accesses non-deterministic operations. During the build, prerendering stops when `Math.random()` is called, deferring to request-time rendering: 102 103 ```tsx filename="app/api/random-number/route.ts" 104 export async function GET() { 105 return Response.json({ 106 randomNumber: Math.random(), 107 }) 108 } 109 ``` 110 111 **Runtime data example** - accesses request-specific data. Prerendering terminates when runtime APIs like `headers()` are called: 112 113 ```tsx filename="app/api/user-agent/route.ts" 114 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 115 116 export async function GET() { 117 const headersList = await headers() 118 const userAgent = headersList.get('user-agent') 119 120 return Response.json({ userAgent }) 121 } 122 ``` 123 124 > **Good to know**: Prerendering stops if the `GET` handler accesses network requests, database queries, async file system operations, request object properties (like `req.url`, `request.headers`, `request.cookies`, `request.body`), runtime APIs like [`cookies()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies), [`headers()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers), [`connection()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection), or non-deterministic operations. 125 126 **Cached example** - accesses dynamic data (database query) but caches it with `use cache`, allowing it to be included in the prerendered response: 127 128 ```tsx filename="app/api/products/route.ts" 129 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 130 131 export async function GET() { 132 const products = await getProducts() 133 return Response.json(products) 134 } 135 136 async function getProducts() { 137 'use cache' 138 cacheLife('hours') 139 140 return await db.query('SELECT * FROM products') 141 } 142 ``` 143 144 > **Good to know**: `use cache` cannot be used directly inside a Route Handler body; extract it to a helper function. Cached responses revalidate according to `cacheLife` when a new request arrives. 145 146 ### Special Route Handlers 147 148 Special Route Handlers like [`sitemap.ts`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap), [`opengraph-image.tsx`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image), and [`icon.tsx`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons), and other [metadata files](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata) remain static by default unless they use Dynamic APIs or dynamic config options. 149 150 ### Route Resolution 151 152 You can consider a `route` the lowest level routing primitive. 153 154 - They **do not** participate in layouts or client-side navigations like `page`. 155 - There **cannot** be a `route.js` file at the same route as `page.js`. 156 157 | Page | Route | Result | 158 | -------------------- | ------------------ | ---------------------------- | 159 | `app/page.js` | `app/route.js` | Conflict | 160 | `app/page.js` | `app/api/route.js` | Valid | 161 | `app/[user]/page.js` | `app/api/route.js` | Valid | 162 163 Each `route.js` or `page.js` file takes over all HTTP verbs for that route. 164 165 ```ts filename="app/page.ts" switcher 166 export default function Page() { 167 return

Hello, Next.js!

168 } 169 170 // Conflict 171 // `app/route.ts` 172 export async function POST(request: Request) {} 173 ``` 174 175 ```js filename="app/page.js" switcher 176 export default function Page() { 177 return

Hello, Next.js!

178 } 179 180 // Conflict 181 // `app/route.js` 182 export async function POST(request) {} 183 ``` 184 185 Read more about how Route Handlers [complement your frontend application](/docs/app/guides/backend-for-frontend), or explore the Route Handlers [API Reference](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route). 186 187 ### Route Context Helper 188 189 In TypeScript, you can type the `context` parameter for Route Handlers with the globally available [`RouteContext`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route#route-context-helper) helper: 190 191 ```ts filename="app/users/[id]/route.ts" switcher 192 import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server' 193 194 export async function GET(_req: NextRequest, ctx: RouteContext<'/users/[id]'>) { 195 const { id } = await ctx.params 196 return Response.json({ id }) 197 } 198 ``` 199 200 > **Good to know** 201 > 202 > - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`. 203

1 --- 2 title: Metadata and OG images 3 description: Learn how to add metadata to your pages and create dynamic OG images. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the Metadata APIs mentioned in this page. 7 links: 8 - app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata 9 - app/api-reference/functions/generate-viewport 10 - app/api-reference/functions/image-response 11 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata 12 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons 13 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image 14 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots 15 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap 16 - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/htmlLimitedBots 17 --- 18 19 The Metadata APIs can be used to define your application metadata for improved SEO and web shareability and include: 20 21 1. [The static `metadata` object](#static-metadata) 22 2. [The dynamic `generateMetadata` function](#generated-metadata) 23 3. Special [file conventions](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata) that can be used to add static or dynamically generated [favicons](#favicons) and [OG images](#static-open-graph-images). 24 25 With all the options above, Next.js will automatically generate the relevant `` tags for your page, which can be inspected in the browser's developer tools. 26 27 The `metadata` object and `generateMetadata` function exports are only supported in Server Components. 28 29 ## Default fields 30 31 There are two default `meta` tags that are always added even if a route doesn't define metadata: 32 33 - The [meta charset tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/meta#attr-charset) sets the character encoding for the website. 34 - The [meta viewport tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Viewport_meta_tag) sets the viewport width and scale for the website to adjust for different devices. 35 36 ```html 37 38 39 ``` 40 41 The other metadata fields can be defined with the `Metadata` object (for [static metadata](#static-metadata)) or the `generateMetadata` function (for [generated metadata](#generated-metadata)). 42 43 ## Static metadata 44 45 To define static metadata, export a [`Metadata` object](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-object) from a static [`layout.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) or [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) file. For example, to add a title and description to the blog route: 46 47 ```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher 48 import type { Metadata } from 'next' 49 50 export const metadata: Metadata = { 51 title: 'My Blog', 52 description: '...', 53 } 54 55 export default function Layout() {} 56 ``` 57 58 ```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher 59 export const metadata = { 60 title: 'My Blog', 61 description: '...', 62 } 63 64 export default function Layout() {} 65 ``` 66 67 You can view a full list of available options, in the [`generateMetadata` documentation](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-fields). 68 69 ## Generated metadata 70 71 You can use [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) function to `fetch` metadata that depends on data. For example, to fetch the title and description for a specific blog post: 72 73 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 74 import type { Metadata, ResolvingMetadata } from 'next' 75 76 type Props = { 77 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 78 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 79 } 80 81 export async function generateMetadata( 82 { params, searchParams }: Props, 83 parent: ResolvingMetadata 84 ): Promise { 85 const slug = (await params).slug 86 87 // fetch post information 88 const post = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${slug}`).then((res) => 89 res.json() 90 ) 91 92 return { 93 title: post.title, 94 description: post.description, 95 } 96 } 97 98 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }: Props) {} 99 ``` 100 101 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 102 export async function generateMetadata({ params, searchParams }, parent) { 103 const slug = (await params).slug 104 105 // fetch post information 106 const post = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${slug}`).then((res) => 107 res.json() 108 ) 109 110 return { 111 title: post.title, 112 description: post.description, 113 } 114 } 115 116 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) {} 117 ``` 118 119 ### Streaming metadata 120 121 For dynamically rendered pages, Next.js streams metadata separately, injecting it into the HTML once `generateMetadata` resolves, without blocking UI rendering. 122 123 Streaming metadata improves perceived performance by allowing visual content to stream first. 124 125 Streaming metadata is **disabled for bots and crawlers** that expect metadata to be in the `` tag (e.g. `Twitterbot`, `Slackbot`, `Bingbot`). These are detected by using the User Agent header from the incoming request. 126 127 You can customize or **disable** streaming metadata completely, with the [`htmlLimitedBots`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/htmlLimitedBots#disabling) option in your Next.js config file. 128 129 Statically rendered pages don’t use streaming since metadata is resolved at build time. 130 131 Learn more about [streaming metadata](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#streaming-metadata). 132 133 ### Memoizing data requests 134 135 There may be cases where you need to fetch the **same** data for metadata and the page itself. To avoid duplicate requests, you can use React's [`cache` function](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) to memoize the return value and only fetch the data once. For example, to fetch the blog post information for both the metadata and the page: 136 137 ```ts filename="app/lib/data.ts" highlight={5} switcher 138 import { cache } from 'react' 139 import { db } from '@/app/lib/db' 140 141 // getPost will be used twice, but execute only once 142 export const getPost = cache(async (slug: string) => { 143 const res = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ where: eq(posts.slug, slug) }) 144 return res 145 }) 146 ``` 147 148 ```js filename="app/lib/data.js" highlight={5} switcher 149 import { cache } from 'react' 150 import { db } from '@/app/lib/db' 151 152 // getPost will be used twice, but execute only once 153 export const getPost = cache(async (slug) => { 154 const res = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ where: eq(posts.slug, slug) }) 155 return res 156 }) 157 ``` 158 159 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 160 import { getPost } from '@/app/lib/data' 161 162 export async function generateMetadata({ 163 params, 164 }: { 165 params: { slug: string } 166 }) { 167 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 168 return { 169 title: post.title, 170 description: post.description, 171 } 172 } 173 174 export default async function Page({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { 175 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 176 return
{post.title}

{post.title}

To add a favicon to your application, create a `favicon.ico` and add to the root of the app folder. 211 212 219 220 > You can also programmatically generate favicons using code. See the [favicon docs](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons) for more information. 221 222 ## Static Open Graph images 223 224 Open Graph (OG) images are images that represent your site in social media. To add a static OG image to your application, create a `opengraph-image.jpg` file in the root of the app folder. 225 226 233 234 You can also add OG images for specific routes by creating a `opengraph-image.jpg` deeper down the folder structure. For example, to create an OG image specific to the `/blog` route, add a `opengraph-image.jpg` file inside the `blog` folder. 235 236 243 244 The more specific image will take precedence over any OG images above it in the folder structure. 245 246 > Other image formats such as `jpeg`, `png`, and `gif` are also supported. See the [Open Graph Image docs](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image) for more information. 247 248 ## Generated Open Graph images 249 250 The [`ImageResponse` constructor](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/image-response) allows you to generate dynamic images using JSX and CSS. With this mechanism, `fetch` calls using `GET` or `HEAD` with the same URL and options in a single render pass are combined into one request. This happens automatically, and you can [opt out](/docs/app/guides/caching#opting-out) by passing an Abort signal to `fetch`. 241 242 Request memoization is scoped to the lifetime of a request. 243 244 You can also deduplicate `fetch` requests by using Next.js’ [Data Cache](/docs/app/guides/caching#data-cache), for example by setting `cache: 'force-cache'` in your `fetch` options. 245 246 Data Cache allows sharing data across the current render pass and incoming requests. 247 248 If you are _not_ using `fetch`, and instead using an ORM or database directly, you can wrap your data access with the [React `cache`](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) function. 249 250 ```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher 251 import { cache } from 'react' 252 import { db, posts, eq } from '@/lib/db' 253 254 export const getPost = cache(async (id: string) => { 255 const post = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ 256 where: eq(posts.id, parseInt(id)), 257 }) 258 }) 259 ``` 260 261 ```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher 262 import { cache } from 'react' 263 import { db, posts, eq } from '@/lib/db' 264 import { notFound } from 'next/navigation' 265 266 export const getPost = cache(async (id) => { 267 const post = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ 268 where: eq(posts.id, parseInt(id)), 269 }) 270 }) 271 ``` 272 273 ## Streaming 274 275 > **Warning:** The content below assumes the [`cacheComponents` config option](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) is enabled in your application. The flag was introduced in Next.js 15 canary. 276 277 When you fetch data in Server Components, the data is fetched and rendered on the server for each request. If you have any slow data requests, the whole route will be blocked from rendering until all the data is fetched. 278 279 To improve the initial load time and user experience, you can use streaming to break up the page's HTML into smaller chunks and progressively send those chunks from the server to the client. 280 281 288 289 There are two ways you can leverage streaming in your application: 290 291 1. Wrapping a page with a [`loading.js` file](#with-loadingjs) 292 2. Wrapping a component with [``](#with-suspense) 293 294 ### With `loading.js` 295 296 You can create a `loading.js` file in the same folder as your page to stream the **entire page** while the data is being fetched. For example, to stream `app/blog/page.js`, add the file inside the `app/blog` folder. 297 298 305 306 ```tsx filename="app/blog/loading.tsx" switcher 307 export default function Loading() { 308 // Define the Loading UI here 309 return
Loading...
310 } 311 ``` 312 313 ```jsx filename="app/blog/loading.js" switcher 314 export default function Loading() { 315 // Define the Loading UI here 316 return
Loading...
317 } 318 ``` 319 320 On navigation, the user will immediately see the layout and a [loading state](#creating-meaningful-loading-states) while the page is being rendered. The new content will then be automatically swapped in once rendering is complete. 321 322 329 330 Behind-the-scenes, `loading.js` will be nested inside `layout.js`, and will automatically wrap the `page.js` file and any children below in a `` boundary. 331 332 339 340 This approach works well for route segments (layouts and pages), but for more granular streaming, you can use ``. 341 342 ### With `` 343 344 `` allows you to be more granular about what parts of the page to stream. For the best user experience, we recommend designing loading states that are meaningful and help users understand the app is responding. For example, you can use skeletons and spinners, or a small but meaningful part of future screens such as a cover photo, title, etc. 397 398 In development, you can preview and inspect the loading state of your components using the [React Devtools](https://react.dev/learn/react-developer-tools). 399 400 ## Examples 401 402 ### Sequential data fetching 403 404 Sequential data fetching happens when one request depends on data from another. 405 406 For example, `` can only fetch data after `` completes because it needs the `artistID`: 407 408 ```tsx filename="app/artist/[username]/page.tsx" switcher 409 export default async function Page({ 410 params, 411 }: { 412 params: Promise<{ username: string }> 413 }) { 414 const { username } = await params 415 // Get artist information 416 const artist = await getArtist(username) 417 418 return ( 419 <> 420

So each segment starts fetching data as soon as possible. 485 486 However, within _any_ component, multiple `async`/`await` requests can still be sequential if placed after the other. {artist.name}

By the time `` is rendered, its data has already been fetched. 575 576 ```tsx filename="app/item/[id]/page.tsx" switcher 577 import { getItem, checkIsAvailable } from '@/lib/data' 578 579 export default async function Page({ 580 params, 581 }: { 582 params: Promise<{ id: string }> 583 }) { 584 const { id } = await params 585 // starting loading item data 586 preload(id) 587 // perform another asynchronous task 588 const isAvailable = await checkIsAvailable() 589 590 return isAvailable ? : null 591 } 592 593 const preload = (id: string) => { 594 // void evaluates the given expression and returns undefined 595 // https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/void 596 void getItem(id) 597 } 598 599 export async function Item({ id }: { id: string }) { 600 const result = await getItem(id) 601 // ... 602 } 603 ``` 604 605 ```jsx filename="app/item/[id]/page.js" switcher 606 import { getItem, checkIsAvailable } from '@/lib/data' 607 608 export default async function Page({ params }) { 609 const { id } = await params 610 // starting loading item data 611 preload(id) 612 // perform another asynchronous task 613 const isAvailable = await checkIsAvailable() 614 615 return isAvailable ? : null 616 } 617 618 const preload = (id) => { 619 // void evaluates the given expression and returns undefined 620 // https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/void 621 void getItem(id) 622 } 623 624 export async function Item({ id }) { 625 const result = await getItem(id) 626 // ... 627 ``` 628 629 Additionally, you can use React's [`cache` function](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) and the [`server-only` package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/server-only) to create a reusable utility function. 14	---
15	
16	Caching is a technique for storing the result of data fetching and other computations so that future requests for the same data can be served faster, without doing the work again. While revalidation allows you to update cache entries without having to rebuild your entire application.
17	
18	Next.js provides a few APIs to handle caching and revalidation. This guide will walk you through when and how to use them.
19	
20	- [`fetch`](#fetch)
21	- [`cacheTag`](#cachetag)
22	- [`revalidateTag`](#revalidatetag)
23	- [`updateTag`](#updatetag)
24	- [`revalidatePath`](#revalidatepath)
25	- [`unstable_cache`](#unstable_cache) (Legacy)
26	
27	## `fetch`
28	
29	By default, [`fetch`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) requests are not cached. You can cache individual requests by setting the `cache` option to `'force-cache'`.
30	
31	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
32	export default async function Page() {
33	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
34	}
35	```
36	
37	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
38	export default async function Page() {
39	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
40	}
41	```
42	
43	> **Good to know**: Although `fetch` requests are not cached by default, Next.js will [pre-render](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) routes that have `fetch` requests and cache the HTML. If you want to guarantee a route is [dynamic](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), use the [`connection` API](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection).
44	
45	To revalidate the data returned by a `fetch` request, you can use the `next.revalidate` option.
46	
47	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
48	export default async function Page() {
49	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
50	}
51	```
52	
53	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
54	export default async function Page() {
55	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
56	}
57	```
58	
59	This will revalidate the data after a specified amount of seconds.
60	
61	You can also tag `fetch` requests to enable on-demand cache invalidation:
62	
63	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
64	export async function getUserById(id: string) {
65	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
66	    next: {
67	      tags: ['user'],
68	    },
69	  })
70	}
71	```
72	
73	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
74	export async function getUserById(id) {
75	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
76	    next: {
77	      tags: ['user'],
78	    },
79	  })
80	}
81	```
82	
83	See the [`fetch` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) to learn more.
84	
85	## `cacheTag`
86	
87	[`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) allows you to tag cached data in [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) so it can be revalidated on-demand. Previously, cache tagging was limited to `fetch` requests, and caching other work required the experimental `unstable_cache` API.
88	
89	With Cache Components, you can use the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive to cache any computation, and `cacheTag` to tag it. This works with database queries, file system operations, and other server-side work.
90	
91	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
92	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
93	
94	export async function getProducts() {
95	  'use cache'
96	  cacheTag('products')
97	
98	  const products = await db.query('SELECT * FROM products')
99	  return products
100	}
101	```
102	
103	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
104	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
105	
106	export async function getProducts() {
107	  'use cache'
108	  cacheTag('products')
109	
110	  const products = await db.query('SELECT * FROM products')
111	  return products
112	}
113	```
114	
115	Once tagged, you can use [`revalidateTag`](#revalidatetag) or [`updateTag`](#updatetag) to invalidate the cache entry for products.
116	
117	> **Good to know**: `cacheTag` is used with [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) and the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive. It expands the caching and revalidation story beyond `fetch`.
118	
119	See the [`cacheTag` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) to learn more.
120	
121	## `revalidateTag`
122	
123	`revalidateTag` is used to revalidate cache entries based on a tag and following an event. The function now supports two behaviors:
124	
125	- **With `profile="max"`**: Uses stale-while-revalidate semantics, serving stale content while fetching fresh content in the background
126	- **Without the second argument**: Legacy behavior that immediately expires the cache (deprecated)
127	
128	After tagging your cached data, using [`fetch`](#fetch) with `next.tags`, or the [`cacheTag`](#cachetag) function, you may call `revalidateTag` in a [Route Handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) or Server Action:
129	
130	```tsx filename="app/lib/actions.ts" highlight={1,5} switcher
131	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
132	
133	export async function updateUser(id: string) {
134	  // Mutate data
135	  revalidateTag('user', 'max') // Recommended: Uses stale-while-revalidate
136	}
137	```
138	
139	```jsx filename="app/lib/actions.js" highlight={1,5} switcher
140	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
141	
142	export async function updateUser(id) {
143	  // Mutate data
144	  revalidateTag('user', 'max') // Recommended: Uses stale-while-revalidate
145	}
146	```
147	
148	You can reuse the same tag in multiple functions to revalidate them all at once.
149	
150	See the [`revalidateTag` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) to learn more.
151	
152	## `updateTag`
153	
154	`updateTag` is specifically designed for Server Actions to immediately expire cached data for read-your-own-writes scenarios. Unlike `revalidateTag`, it can only be used within Server Actions and immediately expires the cache entry.
155	
156	```tsx filename="app/lib/actions.ts" highlight={1,6} switcher
157	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
158	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
159	
160	export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
161	  // Create post in database
162	  const post = await db.post.create({
163	    data: {
164	      title: formData.get('title'),
165	      content: formData.get('content'),
166	    },
167	  })
168	
169	  // Immediately expire cache so the new post is visible
170	  updateTag('posts')
171	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
172	
173	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
174	}
175	```
176	
177	```jsx filename="app/lib/actions.js" highlight={1,6} switcher
178	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
179	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
180	
181	export async function createPost(formData) {
182	  // Create post in database
183	  const post = await db.post.create({
184	    data: {
185	      title: formData.get('title'),
186	      content: formData.get('content'),
187	    },
188	  })
189	
190	  // Immediately expire cache so the new post is visible
191	  updateTag('posts')
192	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
193	
194	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
195	}
196	```
197	
198	The key differences between `revalidateTag` and `updateTag`:
199	
200	- **`updateTag`**: Only in Server Actions, immediately expires cache, for read-your-own-writes
201	- **`revalidateTag`**: In Server Actions and Route Handlers, supports stale-while-revalidate with `profile="max"`
202	
203	See the [`updateTag` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) to learn more.
204	
205	## `revalidatePath`
206	
207	`revalidatePath` is used to revalidate a route and following an event. To use it, call it in a [Route Handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) or Server Action:
208	
209	```tsx filename="app/lib/actions.ts" highlight={1} switcher
210	import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache'
211	
212	export async function updateUser(id: string) {
213	  // Mutate data
214	  revalidatePath('/profile')
215	```
216	
217	```jsx filename="app/lib/actions.js" highlight={1} switcher
218	import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache'
219	
220	export async function updateUser(id) {
221	  // Mutate data
222	  revalidatePath('/profile')
223	```
224	
225	See the [`revalidatePath` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) to learn more.
226	
227	## `unstable_cache`
228	
229	> **Good to know**: `unstable_cache` is an experimental API. We recommend opting into [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) and replacing `unstable_cache` with the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive. See the [Cache Components documentation](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) for more details.
230	
231	`unstable_cache` allows you to cache the result of database queries and other async functions. To use it, wrap `unstable_cache` around the function. For example:
232	
233	```ts filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
234	import { db } from '@/lib/db'
235	export async function getUserById(id: string) {
236	  return db
237	    .select()
238	    .from(users)
239	    .where(eq(users.id, id))
240	    .then((res) => res[0])
241	}
242	```
243	
244	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
245	import { db } from '@/lib/db'
246	
247	export async function getUserById(id) {
248	  return db
249	    .select()
250	    .from(users)
251	    .where(eq(users.id, id))
252	    .then((res) => res[0])
253	}
254	```
255	
256	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={2,11,13} switcher
257	import { unstable_cache } from 'next/cache'
258	import { getUserById } from '@/app/lib/data'
259	
260	export default async function Page({
261	  params,
262	}: {
263	  params: Promise<{ userId: string }>
264	}) {
265	  const { userId } = await params
266	
267	  const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
268	    async () => {
269	      return getUserById(userId)
270	    },
271	    [userId] // add the user ID to the cache key
272	  )
273	}
274	```
275	
276	```jsx filename="app/page.js" highlight={2,7,9} switcher
277	import { unstable_cache } from 'next/cache'
278	import { getUserById } from '@/app/lib/data'
279	
280	export default async function Page({ params }) {
281	  const { userId } = await params
282	
283	  const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
284	    async () => {
285	      return getUserById(userId)
286	    },
287	    [userId] // add the user ID to the cache key
288	  )
289	}
290	```
291	
292	The function accepts a third optional object to define how the cache should be revalidated. It accepts:
293	
294	- `tags`: an array of tags used by Next.js to revalidate the cache.
295	- `revalidate`: the number of seconds after cache should be revalidated.
296	
297	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={6-9} switcher
298	const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
299	  async () => {
300	    return getUserById(userId)
301	  },
302	  [userId],
303	  {
304	    tags: ['user'],
305	    revalidate: 3600,
306	  }
307	)
308	```
309	
310	```jsx filename="app/page.js" highlight={6-9} switcher
311	const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
312	  async () => {
313	    return getUserById(userId)
314	  },
315	  [userId],
316	  {
317	    tags: ['user'],
318	    revalidate: 3600,
319	  }
320	)
321	```
322	
323	See the [`unstable_cache` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unstable_cache) to learn more.

Now I have a thorough understanding of both the existing Pages Router code and the App Router conventions in this version of Next.js 16. Let me plan the migration. Here's my plan: ### Files to create in `app/` directory: 1. **`app/layout.tsx`** — Root layout (replaces `_app.js` and `_document.js`) - Includes ``, ``, global CSS import - Sets up the `AppProvider` context - Adds the header/nav and footer from `_app.js` - Uses `metadata` export for document-level meta tags (replaces `_document.js` Head) 2. **`app/page.tsx`** — Home page (replaces `pages/index.js`) - Server Component with `fetch` instead of `getServerSideProps` - Uses `headers()` from `next/headers` instead of `req.headers['user-agent']` - Uses `generateMetadata` for SEO - Uses `useRouter` for client navigation — but since this is a Server Component, we need to split the client bits 3. **`app/blog/page.tsx`** — Blog index (replaces `pages/blog/index.js`) - Server Component with `fetch` and ISR via `next.revalidate` - Uses metadata export - Client navigation needs to be in a Client Component 4. **`app/blog/[id]/page.tsx`** — Blog post (replaces `pages/blog/[id].js`) - Uses `generateStaticParams` instead of `getStaticPaths` - Uses `fetch` for data with `next.revalidate` instead of `revalidate` return prop - Uses `generateMetadata` for dynamic metadata - Client navigation needs Client Component 5. **`app/not-found.tsx`** — 404 page (replaces `pages/404.js`) 6. **`app/error.tsx`** — Error boundary (replaces `_error.js`) 7. **`app/global-error.tsx`** — Global error (for root layout errors) 8. **`app/api/posts/route.ts`** — API route for posts (replaces `pages/api/posts/index.js`) 9. **`app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts`** — API route for single post (replaces `pages/api/posts/[id].js`) ### Key App Router changes to handle: 1. `getServerSideProps` → Fetch directly in async Server Component 2. `getStaticProps` → Fetch directly in async Server Component 3. `getStaticPaths` → `generateStaticParams` 4. `revalidate` → `next: { revalidate: N }` on fetch calls 5. `_app.js` → `app/layout.tsx` (root layout) 6. `_document.js` → Move to root layout and metadata exports 7. `_error.js` → `error.tsx` and `global-error.tsx` 8. `404.js` → `not-found.tsx` 9. API Route handlers: `(req, res)` → `GET(request, context)`, `POST(request)`, etc. 10. `next/head` → `generateMetadata` export 11. `useRouter` for navigation → need Client Components with `useRouter` from `next/navigation` 12. `params` is a Promise in App Router 13. Use `RouteContext` type helper for API routes Let me now think about the specific files: ### Component Split Strategy Some pages use both server data and client interactivity. In App Router, pages can be Server Components by default, but `useRouter` needs a Client Component. So I need to: - Make the page components Server Components for data fetching - Extract interactive parts (buttons that use `useRouter`) into separate Client Components Actually, looking at this more carefully: 1. **Home page** (`app/page.tsx`): - Fetches data (Server Component), but has a button that uses `useRouter` - I'll create a small client component for the navigation button, and keep the rest as a Server Component - For `headers()`, I'll use `import { headers } from 'next/headers'` 2. **Blog index** (`app/blog/page.tsx`): - Same pattern - data fetching in Server Component - Navigation buttons in Client Component - `revalidate: 60` → `fetch` with `next: { revalidate: 60 }` 3. **Blog post** (`app/blog/[id]/page.tsx`): - `generateStaticParams` for path generation - `isFallback` → use loading.tsx - Navigation in Client Component 4. **404 page** → `app/not-found.tsx` - But useRouter for navigation needs to be Client Component 5. **Error page** → `app/error.tsx` (Client Component) Let me now create all the files. I need to add TypeScript types as requested. Let me plan the exact file structure: ``` app/ layout.tsx → Root layout (html, body, AppProvider, header, footer, globals.css) page.tsx → Home page (Server Component) not-found.tsx → 404 page error.tsx → Error boundary global-error.tsx → Global error boundary blog/ page.tsx → Blog index loading.tsx → Loading state [id]/ page.tsx → Blog post detail loading.tsx → Loading state api/ posts/ route.ts → GET, POST for posts [id]/ route.ts → GET, PUT, DELETE for single post components/ AppProvider.tsx → Keep as-is (but convert to TS) NavButton.tsx → New Client Component for navigation buttons ``` Let me convert everything to TypeScript as requested. Let me think about specific implementation details: ### `app/layout.tsx` ```tsx import type { ReactNode } from 'react'; import type { Metadata } from 'next'; import Link from 'next/link'; import { AppProvider } from '@/components/AppProvider'; import '../styles/globals.css'; export const metadata: Metadata = { title: { default: 'My Blog', template: '%s - My Blog', }, description: 'A complex blog application', icons: { icon: '/favicon.ico', }, }; export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) { return (

My Blog

Home Blog
{children}

© 2024 My Blog

 ); } ``` Wait, but in the root layout, after the `` tag, we don't have `` - we use `` element or metadata. Looking at the docs, the root layout should use `` and then `...`. The metadata is handled by the metadata export. But the fonts link needs to go somewhere. Let me check... Actually, looking at the docs example, the root layout just has `` and `` tags. We can use `` in Next.js 16 with React 19 since it's supported. Or we can use a link tag inside body. Actually wait, looking more carefully at the Next.js docs, the root layout doesn't include Head next/head. The metadata API handles and <meta>. For external stylesheets like Google Fonts, we can either: 1. Use `<link>` in the layout (allowed in React 19) 2. Use the `metadata` API Since the root layout is a Server Component, we can just put `<link>` tags in it directly. Let me now think about the head element. In React 19, you can render `<head>` as a sibling of `<body>` in the root layout. Let me check the example from the docs... From the layout docs: ```tsx export default function DashboardLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { return ( <html lang="en"> <body> <main>{children}</main> </body> </html> ) } ``` There's no `<head>` tag. But in React 19 we can add one. Let me add it for the font preconnect links. Actually, looking at the `_document.js`, it has: - `<Html lang="en">` → root layout uses `<html lang="en">` - `<Head>` with meta description, favicon, font preconnect → metadata export + direct `<head>` or `<link>` in body - `<body>` → same - `<Main />` → `<main>{children}</main>` wrapped in the layout - `<NextScript />` → automatic in App Router OK, let me now write all the files. Let me also think about the client component navigation issue. For the home page (`app/page.tsx`), there's a button that calls `router.push('/blog')`. In App Router, `useRouter` from `next/navigation` can only be used in Client Components. So I'll extract that button into a small Client Component. Similarly, `router.back()` on the blog post page needs a Client Component. And `router.isFallback` check - in App Router, we use `loading.tsx` instead. The "Read More" buttons on the blog index that use `router.push` also need Client Components. Let me create a reusable `NavButton` client component. Actually, let me reconsider - for links, we should prefer `<Link>` over `router.push()` anyway. The `<Link>` component works in Server Components. So: - `router.push('/blog')` → `<Link href="/blog">View All Posts</Link>` or `<button><Link href="/blog">...</Link></button>` - `router.push('/blog/${post.id}')` → `<Link href="/blog/${post.id}">Read More</Link>` - `router.back()` → needs a Client Component For `router.isFallback`, in App Router we use `loading.tsx` instead. Let me now write all files: ### Client Components for navigation I'll create a `NavigateBack` client component: ```tsx 'use client'; import { useRouter } from 'next/navigation'; export function NavigateBack() { const router = useRouter(); return <button onClick={() => router.back()}>← Back</button>; } ``` And for the home page "View All Posts" button, I can just use `<Link>`. OK, let me write everything now. Let me be thorough and create all files. One more thing - for the posts API, looking at the App Router docs, Route Handlers use: ```ts export async function GET(request: Request) {} export async function POST(request: Request) {} ``` And for dynamic routes: ```ts export async function GET(request: Request, { params }: { params: Promise<{ id: string }> }) {} ``` Or using the `RouteContext` helper: ```ts export async function GET(_req: NextRequest, ctx: RouteContext<'/api/posts/[id]'>) { const { id } = await ctx.params ... } ``` Let me use the `RouteContext` pattern for clean types. Now, for error handling in the App Router: - `app/error.tsx` - Client Component that catches errors in the layout and page below it - `app/global-error.tsx` - Client Component that catches errors in the root layout - `app/not-found.tsx` - Shown when `notFound()` is called The original `_error.js` had `getInitialProps` which is a Pages Router API. In App Router: - For 404s → use `not-found.tsx` - For other errors → use `error.tsx` (Client Component with `error` and `reset` props) Let me now create the task list and start writing files. Here are all the files I need to create: 1. `app/layout.tsx` - Root layout 2. `app/page.tsx` - Home page 3. `app/not-found.tsx` - 404 page 4. `app/error.tsx` - Error boundary 5. `app/global-error.tsx` - Global error boundary 6. `app/blog/page.tsx` - Blog index 7. `app/blog/loading.tsx` - Blog loading state 8. `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx` - Blog post detail 9. `app/blog/[id]/loading.tsx` - Blog post loading state 10. `app/api/posts/route.ts` - Posts API 11. `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` - Single post API 12. `components/NavigateBack.tsx` - Client component for back navigation And update: - `components/AppProvider.tsx` → `components/AppProvider.tsx` (rename to .tsx) And then delete the pages directory. Let me also check if there's a `next-env.d.ts` or if I need to create one. Actually, TypeScript conversion note: The `components/AppProvider.js` uses `createContext` and `useState`. I'll convert it to TypeScript. Let me start writing all the files now.

Migration complete. Here's what was done:

## Pages Router → App Router Migration Summary

### Files Created (`app/` directory)

| App Router File | Replaces | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `app/layout.tsx` | `pages/_app.js` + `pages/_document.js` | Root layout with `<html>`, `<body>`, `AppProvider`, header/nav, footer, global CSS import, metadata export, font preconnect links |
| `app/page.tsx` | `pages/index.js` | Async Server Component — `headers()` from `next/headers` replaces `getServerSideProps` `req.headers`, metadata via `generateMetadata` |
| `app/not-found.tsx` | `pages/404.js` | Static not-found page using `<Link>` instead of `router.push` |
| `app/error.tsx` | `pages/_error.js` | Client Component error boundary with `error` and `reset` props (App Router pattern) |
| `app/global-error.tsx` | — | Catches errors in root layout itself, defines its own `<html>`/`<body>` |
| `app/blog/page.tsx` | `pages/blog/index.js` | Async Server Component with `next: { revalidate: 60 }` for ISR |
| `app/blog/loading.tsx` | — | Loading UI replaces `router.isFallback` checks |
| `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx` | `pages/blog/[id].js` | `generateStaticParams` replaces `getStaticPaths`, `next.revalidate` replaces `revalidate` return prop, `generateMetadata` for dynamic metadata |
| `app/blog/[id]/loading.tsx` | — | Post loading state |
| `app/api/posts/route.ts` | `pages/api/posts/index.js` | `GET`/`POST` using Web `Request`/`Response` APIs via `NextResponse.json()` |
| `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` | `pages/api/posts/[id].js` | `GET`/`PUT`/`DELETE` with route context params |

### Client Components Created

- **`components/NavigateBack.tsx`** — `'use client'` component wrapping `useRouter().back()` for blog post back navigation
- **`components/AppProvider.tsx`** — Converted from `.js` to `.tsx` with proper TypeScript types

### Key API Changes Applied

| Pages Router (old) | App Router (new) |
|---|---|
| `getServerSideProps({ req })` | `headers()` in Server Component |
| `getStaticProps` + `revalidate` | `fetch(..., { next: { revalidate: 60 } })` in async Server Component |
| `getStaticPaths` | `generateStaticParams` |
| `next/head` + `<Head>` | `export const metadata` / `generateMetadata` |
| `next/router` + `useRouter` | `next/navigation` + `useRouter` (client), `<Link>` (server) |
| `(req, res) =>` API handlers | `GET(request, context)`, `POST(request)` with `NextResponse` |
| `_app.js` / `_document.js` | Root `layout.tsx` |
